name: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.

profile.description: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. is the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America, operating an integrated steel supply chain from iron ore mining through steelmaking and finishing. The company serves automotive, infrastructure, and industrial markets with approximately 16 million tons of annual steel shipments.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Cleveland-Cliffs operates the world's most modern Direct Reduction plant in Toledo, Ohio (operational since November 2020), and has completed hydrogen injection trials at its Middletown and Indiana Harbor blast furnaces [1]. However, the company canceled a green steel project in Middletown, Ohio, sending mixed signals about its commitment to next-generation steelmaking [3]. Innovation efforts are focused on process improvements rather than transformative technology, and there is no evidence of meaningful AI adoption in operations.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: CEO Lourenco Goncalves has built a distinctive pro-union culture, stating 'it's very easy to be a bad company when you don't have a union,' and the company has successfully negotiated labor contracts covering thousands of employees [2]. Goncalves received the S&P Global Platts CEO/Chairperson of the Year Global Metals Award and the Willy Korf Steel Vision Award, reflecting industry respect [2]. However, Glassdoor reviews and the confrontational style that includes publicly berating analysts and short-sellers can be polarizing.

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: Cleveland-Cliffs posted a $1.4 billion net loss in 2025 with adjusted EBITDA collapsing to just $37 million from $773 million in 2024, and debt-to-equity stands at a concerning 126% [1]. Revenue declined 3% YoY to $18.6 billion, and ROE is deeply negative at -21.6%. While management guides to $500 million EBITDA improvement in 2026 via slab contract termination and $310 million in cost savings, the stock has fallen 28% from its SMA-50 of $11.59, reflecting market skepticism about the turnaround [1].

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI is neither a major threat nor a significant enabler for Cleveland-Cliffs' core steelmaking operations. The heavy industrial nature of blast furnace operations limits AI disruption risk, though AI-powered process optimization and predictive maintenance could offer marginal efficiency gains [2]. The company's competitive moat derives from vertical integration, union relationships, and tariff protection rather than technology — making it relatively insulated from AI disruption but also unlikely to benefit meaningfully from AI tailwinds.

infinite_game.summary: Cleveland-Cliffs demonstrates infinite game characteristics through its aggressive vertical integration strategy and pro-union culture, building long-term competitive advantages in domestic steel production [2]. However, the cancellation of the Middletown green steel project and heavy reliance on tariff protection suggest a company that may be playing to win the current trade policy cycle rather than building for a genuinely sustainable future [3].

just_cause.analysis: Goncalves frames Cleveland-Cliffs' mission around revitalizing American manufacturing and providing good union jobs, which resonates as a genuine purpose [2]. The company's 25% GHG emissions intensity reduction ahead of schedule demonstrates environmental commitment, though the Cleveland Works facility remains the largest single CO2 emitter in five Ohio counties [3].

trusting_teams.analysis: Cleveland-Cliffs' pro-union stance and successful negotiation of labor contracts across numerous operations creates genuine trust with the workforce [2]. CEO Goncalves's emphasis on workforce investment, training, and development has been recognized with industry leadership awards. The union partnership covering 1,700+ hourly employees at Cleveland Works alone exemplifies this commitment [3].

worthy_rivals.analysis: Cleveland-Cliffs competes against Nucor, Steel Dynamics, and U.S. Steel domestically, and has positioned itself as the dominant flat-rolled steel supplier to the automotive sector through multi-year fixed-price contracts [1]. The attempted $7.2 billion U.S. Steel acquisition and ongoing POSCO negotiations show willingness to compete aggressively for industry leadership [1].

existential_flexibility.analysis: The transformation from an iron ore miner to a vertically integrated steel producer through four major acquisitions since 2020 demonstrates significant existential flexibility [2]. However, the cancellation of the Middletown green steel project and heavy dependence on Section 232 tariffs at 50% suggest the company may struggle to adapt if trade policy shifts or if the industry transitions more rapidly to electric arc furnace steelmaking [3].

courage_to_lead.analysis: Goncalves has shown bold leadership through transformative acquisitions and willingness to publicly advocate for American steel, including confronting analysts and short-sellers [2]. However, idling the Dearborn blast furnace and Hibbing mine to cut costs, while strategically sound, signals retrenchment rather than forward-looking investment during a challenging period [1].

investment_thesis: Cleveland-Cliffs offers a compelling turnaround thesis at a steep discount, trading at 4.5x EV/EBITDA versus the 6.2x steel sector average, with multiple catalysts converging in 2026 [1]. The termination of a low-margin slab supply contract is expected to add $500 million in EBITDA, combined with $310 million in annual cost savings from facility idling and a $60/ton price increase in Q1 2026 [1]. Section 232 tariffs at 50% and melted-and-poured requirements provide a structural demand tailwind, while multi-year fixed-price contracts with all major automotive OEMs secure high-margin volumes for 2026 and beyond [1]. The POSCO definitive agreement, targeted for H1 2026, and $425 million in asset sale proceeds provide additional upside optionality.
thesis_continued: The bear case is severe: a $1.4 billion net loss in 2025 with EBITDA collapsing 95% to just $37 million reveals the fragility of a capital-intensive, cyclical business with 126% debt-to-equity [1]. Cleveland-Cliffs has been characterized as the 'worst house in a troubled neighborhood' by skeptical analysts, and the stock's 28% decline from its 50-day SMA reflects eroding confidence [1]. Any reversal of Section 232 tariffs under a future administration would eliminate the primary demand driver. The Canadian counter-tariff on steel imports directly impacts the Stelco subsidiary acquired in November 2024, and environmental advocates are pressuring the company over coal-dependent operations at Cleveland Works, the largest CO2 emitter in five Ohio counties [3]. The path from $37 million EBITDA to the targeted $500 million requires flawless execution on cost cuts and pricing that has yet to be demonstrated.
key_catalysts: Slab contract termination expected to add ~$500 million in EBITDA in 2026 by eliminating below-market sales that depressed margins throughout 2025 [1]
POSCO definitive agreement targeted for H1 2026 could unlock strategic value through partnership or divestiture of non-core assets [1]
Section 232 tariffs at 50% and melted-and-poured requirements are driving domestic steel demand, with Q1 2026 pricing already up ~$60/ton [1]
key_risks: Debt-to-equity of 126% creates significant financial fragility — if the 2026 EBITDA recovery falls short of $500 million target, the company could face covenant pressure or credit downgrades [1]
Section 232 tariff reversal or reduction under a future administration would collapse the domestic steel pricing premium that underpins the entire turnaround thesis [1]

Environmental regulatory risk from Cleveland Works, the largest CO2 emitter in five Ohio counties — potential carbon pricing or stricter EPA emissions standards could impose material compliance costs on coal-dependent blast furnace operations [3]
sources: Cleveland-Cliffs Q4 2025 Earnings Release and Transcript, Manufacturing Dive — 'Cleveland-Cliffs looks to recover after challenging 2025', February 2026
Fortune — 'Who is Lourenco Goncalves?', City Club of Cleveland — 'Man of Steel' interview, February 2025
Cleveland-Cliffs 2024 Sustainability Report, Signal Cleveland — 'City of Cleveland climate goals require green steel', Industrious Labs report, accessed March 2026
